25.42: Abigail's rapid departure — 'she rose quickly' (wattāqom ʾăḇîgayil wattᵉmahēr) — mirrors the narrator's earlier emphasis on her speed (v18 — 'moved quickly'). Her five serving women (ḥāmēš naʿărōtêhā) accompany her, appropriate for a woman of her status traveling to a new household. The detail that she mounted her donkey and followed David's messengers is consistent with the social protocols established throughout the chapter.
25.43: The notice that David had also taken Ahinoam of Jezreel (ʾăḥînōʿam hayizrᵉʿēlit) as a wife is inserted without ceremony — she appears only here and in 27:3 and 30:5. Jezreel was a Judahite town in the Negeb (Josh 15:56), not the famous northern Jezreel valley. The polygamous arrangement — two wives simultaneously — was legally permissible within the Israelite system and was standard practice for a warrior chieftain of David's status. The narrator registers it without editorial comment, though the Deuteronomic law of kingship (Deut 17:17 — 'he shall not multiply wives for himself') creates a retroactive context of concern.
25.44: Saul's disposal of Michal — giving her to Palti son of Laish from Gallim — is a calculated political act. Michal was the daughter through whom David had a marriage-claim to the royal house; by reassigning her, Saul attempts to sever David's dynastic connection and publicly humiliate him. Gallim was a town in Benjamin (Isa 10:30), keeping Michal within Saul's political sphere. Paltiel/Palti son of Laish is unknown outside of this and 2 Sam 3:14-16 (where his grief at Michal's return to David is vividly described). The notice frames the legal complication that will arise when David later demands Michal's return (2 Sam 3:14) as the condition of his covenant with Abner.

26.1: The Ziphites' second betrayal of David — they had previously gone to Saul at Gibeah (23:19) with the same intelligence about David's location on the hill of Hachilah. Whether this represents a second incident or an alternative telling of the same event has been debated since antiquity (Ellicott notes Ewald's doubts), but the narrative presents it as a distinct episode: the geography differs, the outcome differs, and the theological point is distinct. Jeshimon (hayyᵉšîmōn) literally means 'the waste, the desolation' — a name for the most arid part of the Judean wilderness east of the central ridge, overlooking the Dead Sea basin. Hachilah is in this area.
26.2: Saul's deployment of 3,000 'chosen men of Israel' (ʾîš bāḥûr miyyiśrāʾēl) — the same number as his force at En-gedi (24:2) — represents a substantial portion of his standing army. The wilderness of Ziph east of Jeshimon is some of the most inhospitable terrain in the Judean wilderness — deep wadis, bare limestone, limited water. Military movement in this terrain required local knowledge, which is presumably why the Ziphites function as guides. David's 600 men, who knew this terrain intimately, would have had significant advantages over a larger conventional force in such conditions.
26.3: The geography of the encounter: Saul pitched camp on Hachilah while David watched from the wilderness nearby — a surveillance position. David's scouts confirm Saul's arrival (v4) before he acts, consistent with the careful intelligence practice seen throughout the wilderness period. The reference to 'the road' (ʾal pᵉnê derek, along the road east of Jeshimon) suggests a track through the wilderness, possibly the route from the Judean highlands toward the Dead Sea used by travelers and military forces.
26.4: David's intelligence network — sending scouts who return with confirmation — is a standard military precaution. Throughout the wilderness period David is portrayed as consistently better informed than Saul (cf. 23:7 — Saul received intelligence about David's location; 23:25-26 — the near-capture at Maon; 26:3-4 — David watching Saul's camp). This intelligence advantage is one of the narrative's recurring demonstrations that YHWH is with David and that David exercises wisdom in his wilderness campaign.
26.5: David's night reconnaissance of Saul's camp — approaching to see where Saul and Abner son of Ner lay (wayāqom dawid wayyāšob ʾel hammāqôm ʾăšer šāḵab šām šāʾûl) — reveals the Israelite military camp layout: the king at the center surrounded by his chariot troops (hammaʿgāl, the wagon-circle or baggage enclosure), with Abner, the army commander, sleeping beside him. The wagon-circle enclosure was standard Ancient Near Eastern field camp organization, creating a defensive perimeter for the command nucleus. Abner son of Ner was Saul's cousin (14:50-51) and the professional head of the military — his placement beside the king represents the closest protective ring.
26.6: The roster of David's inner circle at this moment — Ahimelech the Hittite and Abishai son of Zeruiah — is historically significant. Ahimelech the Hittite appears only here; his presence indicates that David's force included non-Israelite warriors, consistent with the pattern of the later Davidic court (Uriah the Hittite, the Cherethites and Pelethites). Abishai son of Zeruiah is David's nephew through his sister Zeruiah (1 Chron 2:16) — one of the three sons of Zeruiah (Joab, Abishai, Asahel) who form the most militarily capable and most morally problematic element of David's inner circle. Abishai's volatility will be a recurring feature of David's career: capable of extraordinary courage, but requiring restraint from the king in matters of bloodshed.
26.7: The detail that Saul slept with his spear 'stuck in the ground at his head' (wᵉḥănîtô muṣṣeḇet bāʾāreṣ mᵉraʾăšōtāyw) is culturally loaded: the spear (ḥănît) was the primary symbol of royal authority in early Israel. At Gibeah, Saul had brandished his spear while sitting enthroned (18:10; 19:9); here it stands upright beside his sleeping head as a mark of royal identity visible to any observer. The water jug (ṣappāḥat hammayim) beside it is equally emblematic — in the desert context, control of water was a mark of power. David takes both (v11-12), the spear and the jug, as tokens of his access to the king's person.
26.8: Abishai's offer to kill Saul — 'God has delivered your enemy into your hand today' — uses the same theological language as David's men at En-gedi (24:4). The argument that a single spear-blow would suffice ('I will pin him to the ground at once with the spear and will not need to strike twice') is the calculation of a professional soldier: one blow through the sleeping man's chest with his own spear, pressed through to the ground. Abishai frames this as YHWH's provision — the theological cover for killing the LORD's anointed. David's refusal demonstrates that he understands the theology more deeply than his most capable warrior.
26.9: 'Who can stretch out his hand against the LORD's anointed and be guiltless?' (mî šālaḥ yāḏô biMᵉšîaḥ YHWH wᵉniqqāh) — the theological principle David articulates is the inalienability of the divine anointing. Māšîaḥ (the LORD's anointed) is not merely an honorific but a covenantal status: the king has been set apart by divine commissioning and cannot be removed by human violence without incurring guilt. David consistently applies this principle to Saul throughout the wilderness period (24:6-7; 26:9, 11, 23), even though he himself is the divinely designated successor. The principle protects the institution of divine kingship, not merely Saul's person.
26.10: David's three alternatives for Saul's death — 'the LORD will strike him, or his appointed time will come and he will die, or he will go down to battle and be swept away' (yiggāpennû ʾō yôm môtô wᵉyāmōt ʾô bammilḥāmāh yēreḏ wᵉnispāh) — constitute a theology of divinely administered mortality. The three scenarios cover natural death by divine agency, death by old age, and death in battle. All three will be fulfilled in some combination at Jezreel (ch. 31): Saul dies in battle, wounded by Philistine arrows, and delivers the final blow himself. David's restraint is not passivity but confidence in YHWH's timing.
26.11: 'The LORD forbid that I should stretch out my hand against the LORD's anointed' (ḥālîlāh llî mêYHWH mišlōaḥ yāḏî biMᵉšîaḥ YHWH) — the phrase ḥālîlāh llî is the strongest negative oath formula in Hebrew: 'Far be it from me,' an expression of profound repudiation. David's taking of the spear and water jug rather than Saul's life demonstrates the principle in action: he has the access, the opportunity, and the capable assistant — and he takes only the symbols, not the life. The spear and water jug will serve as evidence of David's restraint and proximity when he presents them to Saul from a distance (vv16, 22).
26.12: 'A deep sleep from the LORD had fallen upon them' (tardēmāh YHWH nāpᵉlāh ʿălêhem) — the tardēmāh (deep sleep, trance) is the same word used for the sleep that fell on Adam at the creation of Eve (Gen 2:21) and for the deep sleep of Abram at the covenant ceremony (Gen 15:12). In each case it marks a divine action that the human participant cannot witness or resist — the normal cognitive defenses are suspended. The narrative is explicit: the sleep is not natural but divinely caused. No one 'saw, or knew, or awakened' — YHWH provided the access; David chose not to use it for violence.
26.13: David's withdrawal to the opposite hill — 'a great distance between them' (hammerḥāq rāḇ bênêhem) — before calling out to the camp is tactically sound and rhetorically calculated: he needs the physical distance to ensure he is not captured immediately, and the distance means his call across the valley will be heard by the whole camp, turning the confrontation into a public spectacle that maximizes the evidence of his restraint and Abner's failure. The opposite ridge would have been visible across the wadi in the early morning light.
26.14: David's call to Abner invokes the honor-shame dynamic of military responsibility: 'You are a man, are you not? And who is like you in Israel?' — this flattery is ironic, immediately undercut by 'why then have you not guarded your lord the king?' Abner's duty as military commander was precisely the physical protection of the king's person. David's challenge is a public humiliation: the greatest soldier in Israel failed in his primary duty, and a fugitive outlaw was able to penetrate to the king's side while the whole army slept. Abner's failure will be publicly visible to every man in the camp at first light.
26.15: David's rhetorical question — 'Is this not good what you have done?' — is sarcastic: clearly it is not. 'As the LORD lives, you all deserve to die (bᵉnê māwet ʾattem, sons of death) for not guarding your lord the king, the LORD's anointed' — David's indictment is legally framed. Failure to guard the king's person was a capital offense in ancient Near Eastern military law; Abner and the camp guards have committed a dereliction deserving death under the standards of their own military code. David's rhetorical position is brilliant: he is the fugitive outlaw, but he is the one upholding the standards the king's own army has violated.
26.16: The invitation to 'look around — where is the king's spear and the water jug that was at his head?' produces the physical evidence at dawn: the camp can see that the royal emblems are gone. The removal of the spear is simultaneously a demonstration of access, a symbolic act of power (taking the king's weapons), and a gesture of restraint (the king himself was not harmed). The rhetorical structure is designed so that Saul and the whole camp must acknowledge both David's capability and his choice not to use it.
26.17: Saul's recognition of David's voice — 'Is that your voice, David my son?' (ăhăzeh qôlᵉḵā bᵉnî dāwiḏ) — returns to the 'my son' formula that recurs in their confrontations (24:16) and carries its painful irony: Saul addresses as 'son' the man he has been trying to kill. The term of endearment in the midst of pursuit marks the psychological complexity of Saul's relationship to David — genuine affection and genuine murderous intent coexist in a person whose rational faculties have been increasingly overwhelmed by the spirit of fear (18:12).
26.18: David's legal self-defense — 'What have I done? What evil is in my hand?' (meh ʿāśîtî ûmah bᵉyāḏî rāʿāh) — restates the forensic argument from ch. 24. The challenge is rhetorical: David knows he has done nothing; he wants Saul to acknowledge it publicly. The persistent pattern of David's legal self-defense in the wilderness period — appealing to witnesses, presenting evidence, invoking YHWH as judge — constructs him as a man seeking legal vindication rather than personal revenge, contrasting sharply with the arbitrary violence Saul has been directing against him and the Nob priests.
26.19: 'If the LORD has moved you against me, let him accept an offering' (ʾim YHWH hᵉsîtᵉḵā ḇî yārōaḥ minḥāh) — David's hypothetical acknowledgment that YHWH might have incited Saul against him reflects the theology of divine testing through human agency (cf. Job's suffering as YHWH-permitted). The reference to men who have 'driven me away from sharing in the LORD's inheritance, saying “Go, serve other gods”’ (ʾamrû lēḵ ʿăḇōḏ ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm) expresses the ANE understanding of territorial religion: being expelled from the land of Israel meant being cut off from YHWH's territorial domain and compelled to serve the gods of whatever land you fled to. For David to be forced into Philistine territory would mean — under this theology — being handed over to Philistine gods. David is appealing to YHWH as a territorial deity who should be interested in keeping his servant within the holy land.
26.20: 'Do not let my blood fall on the ground far from the presence of the LORD' (ʾal yippōl dāmî ʾarṣāh minnegeḏ penê YHWH) — dying in a foreign land, away from YHWH's presence, was a feared fate in the ANE framework: burial away from one's tribal and covenantal community meant disconnection from the community of the dead as well as the living. The comparison of Saul to a man hunting a single flea or a partridge in the mountains (kᵉrōḏēp haqōrēʾ bᵉhārîm) — the partridge was proverbially difficult to catch in the rocky terrain — invokes both the absurdity of the disproportion and David's continued reliance on the wilderness geography that has protected him.
26.21: Saul's confession — 'I have sinned' (ḥāṭāʾtî) — is his second such confession in the narrative (the first was 15:24, under Samuel's judgment). As at 15:24, the confession is elicited by public exposure rather than internal conviction, and as there, it is immediately followed by a request that David 'return' without any structural change in the situation. Saul's confessions have a liturgical quality — he speaks the right words — but the behavior continues. The contrast with David's own admissions of fault (e.g., the heart-struck reaction at En-gedi in 24:5) marks a difference in spiritual character: David's conscience is operative; Saul's confession is performative.
26.22: David's offer to return the spear — 'let one of your young men come across and take it' — refuses the reconciliation Saul has just proposed while honoring the letter of respect: he is returning the royal emblem without personally returning to Saul's power. The refusal to cross to Saul's side is tactically obvious (it would mean capture) but also symbolically significant: the physical distance between them represents the irreversible separation that has been established by Saul's violence against the Nob priests and his persistent pursuit of YHWH's anointed.
26.23: 'The LORD rewards every man for his righteousness and faithfulness' (YHWH yāšîb lāʾîš ʾet ṣiḏqātô wᵉʾet ʾĕmûnātô) — David's closing theological statement encapsulates the covenant-faithfulness ethic that has governed his choices in the wilderness: ṣeḏāqāh (righteousness — conformity to covenant standards) and ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness/reliability — consistency of character) are the two covenant virtues that YHWH is expected to reward. David's refusal to strike the anointed king is presented as an act of ṣeḏāqāh — righteous conduct — that YHWH will vindicate.
26.24: 'As I valued your life today, may the LORD value my life and rescue me from all distress' (kᵉʾăšer gāḏᵉlāh napšᵉḵā baʿênay hayyôm hazzeh kēn tiḏgal napšî bᵉʿênê YHWH) — the structure is a formal retribution prayer: the measure of value David gave to Saul's life should be the measure YHWH gives to David's life. This is covenant theology in its most direct form: the human actor aligns his behavior with YHWH's standards in expectation of corresponding treatment from YHWH. It is not manipulation but the articulation of covenant mutuality.
26.25: Saul's final spoken words to David — 'Blessed be you, my son David! You will accomplish great things (gam pāʿōl tipʿal — you will certainly do) and will surely prevail' — constitute an involuntary prophecy from the one who has been trying to prevent David's success. Saul's blessing at this moment is the narrative's way of sealing the divine inevitability: even the enemy of YHWH's chosen acknowledges his election. The parties separate permanently — this is the last meeting of Saul and David in the narrative. They go their separate ways: Saul to Gibeah, David to his path toward the kingship.

27.1: David's inner monologue — 'I am going to die by Saul's hand one of these days; my best option is to escape into the land of the Philistines' (ʾēsāpēh yôm ʾeḥāḏ bᵉyaḏ šāʾûl ʾên ṭôḇ lî kî māôṣ ʾimmālaṭ ʾel ʾereṣ pᵉlištîm) — is a rare direct window into David's psychology. The decision to seek Philistine patronage is made not from faith but from despair of Saul's persistence, and the narrator presents it without direct comment. The theological implication — that David's choice to seek Philistine protection is a lapse from the trust in YHWH that characterized his wilderness period — is left for the reader to infer from the context of the preceding chapters. Ironically, Saul will stop pursuing David once he crosses the border (v4), making David's calculation partially correct.
27.2: David's crossing to Achish son of Maoch, king of Gath (ʾākîš ben mākôḵ melek gat) — this is a second approach to Gath (the first was the feigned madness episode of 21:10-15), but under entirely different circumstances. David now arrives not as a lone fugitive but as a military chieftain with 600 men — a substantial force whose value as mercenaries or allies would not be lost on a Philistine king. The shift from individual refugee to client warlord represents a change in David's bargaining position that the narrative carefully registers.
27.3: David's 'settlement' at Gath (wayyēšeḇ dāwiḏ ʿim ʾākîš bᵉgat) with his entire company — men, households, two wives — represents a formal integration into the Philistine political structure as a client. The mention of his wives — Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail, Nabal's widow from Carmel — provides continuity with the preceding chapter and marks the domestic reality of David's military band: these are not soldiers but a community in exile, with families whose welfare David is responsible for. The settlement at Gath proper (the Philistine urban center) is temporary — the more significant settlement is at Ziklag (vv5-6).
27.4: Saul's cessation of pursuit once David has crossed into Philistine territory confirms what David calculated (v1): the territorial logic of the period meant that Saul's military authority did not extend beyond Israelite-controlled land. The Philistine cities of the coastal plain and Shephelah were under Philistine control; a military incursion to retrieve a fugitive there would have been an act of war against Philistia. David had in effect used the international border as a protection that YHWH's wilderness geography could not fully guarantee.
27.5: David's request for a separate town — 'why should your servant live in the royal city with you?' — is diplomatically sophisticated. His stated reason ('I am not worthy to dwell in the royal city with you') is courtly humility (māh ʿabdᵉḵā kî tēšēḇ bᵉqiryat hammelukāh ʿimmāḵ), but his actual reason (v11 — he does not want Achish's men to learn of his raiding activities) requires distance from Achish's court. An outlying town would give David territorial authority, operational independence, and the ability to conduct raids without Philistine surveillance. The request is the standard petition of a junior vassal to a suzerain for a fief-town — Achish grants it as a standard patronage allocation.
27.6: Ziklag (ṣiqlāg) was allocated to David 'that day.' The editorial note — 'that is why Ziklag has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day' (lāḵēn hāyᵉtāh ṣiqlāg lᵉmalkê yᵉhûḏāh ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh) — is a temporal marker indicating the narrator's own period (presumably early monarchy) and confirms the historicity of the tradition: Ziklag was still under Judahite crown ownership in the narrator's time, traceable back to David's Philistine sojourn. Ziklag's location is debated — Tell esh-Sharia (Tell Halif area, Beersheba basin) is the most widely accepted identification, approximately 25 km northeast of Beer-sheba. This places it in the Negeb fringe, between Philistine and Israelite territory — a borderland perfectly suited to David's ambiguous position.
27.7: The chronological note — 'a year and four months' (yāmîm ʾarbaʿāh ʿāśār ḥōḏeš, literally 'a year and four months of days') — is precise and unusual: the narrator knows exactly how long David spent in Philistine territory. The 16-month period encompasses the raids described in vv8-12 and ends with the Philistine muster before the battle of Jezreel (ch. 29). The precision suggests access to a reliable chronological source.
27.8: David's raiding targets — the Geshurites (hagᵉšûrî), Girzites (hagᵉrzî), and Amalekites (hāʿămālēqî) — were semi-nomadic peoples inhabiting the southern fringe of the Negeb and Sinai, described as occupying the land 'from of old as you go toward Shur and as far as the land of Egypt.' Geshur here is the Negeb Geshur (distinct from the Aramaean Geshur near the Sea of Galilee); the Girzites are otherwise unknown; the Amalekites were Israel's oldest wilderness enemy (Exod 17:8-16; 1 Sam 15). Raiding these populations was consistent with David's role as a Judahite defender of the southern border — these were the peoples who raided Israelite settlements. David was simultaneously serving his Philistine patron (appearing to raid against Judah, v10) and actually raiding traditional enemies of Israel.
27.9: The policy of leaving no survivors — 'David did not leave a man or a woman alive' (wᵉlōʾ yᵉḥayyeh dawid ʾîš wᵉʾiššāh) — is brutal and is presented without editorial softening. The stated reason (v11) is operational security: no survivors means no informants reaching Gath. The taking of flocks, oxen, donkeys, camels, and clothing is standard Iron Age raiding practice; the goods become the spoil (šālāl) that David returns to Achish as tribute and proof of loyalty. The total destruction policy echoes the ḥērem (ban/devotion) applied to Canaanite populations in the conquest narratives, though here the motivation is concealment rather than theological dedication.
27.10: Achish's query — 'Where did you raid today?' — and David's false reply — 'Against the Negeb of Judah,' or 'Against the Negeb of the Jerahmeelites,' or 'Against the Negeb of the Kenites' — constitute a systematic deception. David names Israelite-affiliated territories (Judah, the Jerahmeelites, the Kenites — all groups within the southern Israelite orbit) while actually raiding non-Israelite populations to the south and west. Achish interprets this as David burning his bridges with his own people — making him permanently dependent on Philistine protection. The deception is narratively effective but morally complex: David is lying to his patron while technically protecting his own people.
27.11: 'David would take neither man nor woman alive to bring back to Gath, thinking “lest they tell about us, saying: So David has done”' — the operational security logic is explicit and chilling: the massacres are the direct result of the deception. If survivors reached Gath they could contradict David's false reports. The narrative does not justify this; it records it. This is the darkest moral period of David's career — the deception of Achish required ongoing violence against civilian populations to maintain the fiction. The 'throughout the time he lived in the country of the Philistines' (kol yᵉmê šiḇtô biśᵉḏēh pᵉlištiym) confirms this was not an isolated incident but a sustained policy.
27.12: Achish's conclusion — 'he has made himself utterly repulsive to his own people Israel, so he will be my servant forever' (hibʾîš hibʾîš bᵉʿammô bᵉyiśrāʾēl wᵉhāyâ lî lᵉʾeḇeḏ ʿôlām) — captures the Philistine king's perspective perfectly: he believes David has permanently alienated himself from Israel and therefore has no option but permanent Philistine clientage. The irony is total: Achish's confident conclusion is entirely wrong. David will return to rule Israel; the 'permanent alienation' Achish sees is the temporary cover of deception. But the narrative leaves the reader aware of the cost: David has gained operational freedom by becoming a liar to his patron — a tension that will come to a head in ch. 29 when Achish expects David to fight against Israel.
